privacy.s: 1 publicly-disclosed data breach per Have I Been Pwned (2021) — 47M accounts compromised. Most recent: T-Mobile (2021).

wiki.controversies:
== Controversies ==
=== Sidekick data outage ===

On October 1, 2009, some users of Microsoft's Sidekick handset temporarily lost personal data, including contacts, notes, and calendars. On October 8, most data services were restored to users. The company and Microsoft announced on October 10 that Sidekick device data "almost certainly has been lost as a result of a server failure at Microsoft/Danger." On October 15, Microsoft said it had been able to recover most or all data and would begin to restore it.

=== Network outages ===
On November 9, 2009, some of the company's subscribers temporarily lost the ability to send and receive calls and text messages for several hours. The company confirmed the outage via Twitter. The company stated that approximately five percent of its subscribers had been affected. It claimed that the problem was caused by a system software error.

On May 8, 2018, subscribers in Houston, Texas, experienced a four-hour service interruption caused by damage to a fiber-optic cable.

On June 15, 2020, subscribers across the United States suffered a service outage (primarily voice and text) due to routing issues.

On February 13, 2023, T-Mobile users across the United States experienced widespread network outages. The company quickly responded that they were looking to address the disruption that caused the cellular service outage.

=== Misrepresentation as 4G ===
In 2010, T-Mobile began marketing both its HSPA and HSPA+ services as "4G". Media outlets called this branding deceptive.

After the ITU expanded its definition of 4G to include HSPA+, T-Mobile continued to market standard HSPA devices and service as 4G. Not only do these HSPA (non-Evolved) devices continue to not meet 4G standards, they are incapable of operating at 4G speeds. Concerns were also displayed over the possibility of confusion when actual LTE networks were deployed.

=== Information security ===
Nicolas Jacobsen was charged with intruding into the company's internal network in January 2005. Reports indicated that for about a year Jacobsen had access to customer passwords, e-mail, address books, Social Security numbers, birth dates, and Sidekick photos. Affected customers included members of the United States Secret Service. Secret Service informant identified Jacobsen as part of [https://web.archive.org/web/20080916201228/http://www.ustreas.gov/usss/press/pub2304.pdf "Operation Firewall"] which provided evidence that Jacobsen had attempted to sell customer information to others for identity theft. T-Mobile USA and the Secret Service did not elaborate on the methods Jacobsen used to gain access but sources close to the case indicated that Jacobsen exploited an unpatched flaw in the Oracle WebLogic Server application software used by the company. Additional SQL injection vulnerabilities with the company's web site were reported by Jack Koziol of the InfoSec Institute.

T-Mobile offers access to voicemail without the input of a password by default. Parties acting in bad faith may be able to access such voice mailboxes via Caller ID spoofing. To avoid this possibility, T-Mobile recommends that all customers password-protect their mailboxes, but still offers the no password configuration by default due to customer demand.

On June 6, 2009, a message posted from an email account "pwnmobile_at_Safe-mail.net" to the Full Disclosure mailing list claimed that the company's network had been breached and showed sample data. The sender offered "databases, confidential documents, scripts and programs from their servers, financial documents up to 2009" to the highest bidder. On June 9, the company issued a statement confirming the breach but stating that customer data was safe. It claimed to have identified the source document for the sample data and believe it was not obtained by hacking. A later statement claimed that there was not any evidence of a breach.

=== Privacy and surveillance ===
T-Mobile USA received a portion of the 1.3 million large
wiki.extract: T-Mobile US, Inc. is an American wireless network operator headquartered in Bellevue, Washington. Its majority shareholder and namesake is the German telecommunications company Deutsche Telekom. T-Mobile is the second largest wireless carrier in the United States, with 140 million subscribers as of September 30, 2025.

sampleRecords.event_description: Slip, trip, stumble on same level  without fall
sampleRecords.final_narrative: An employee was responding to an emergency call at the end of a pier when they stepped into a gap in the pier boards, resulting in a broken tibia, fibula, and ankle.

sampleRecords.employer: Waste Management Mobile Bay Environmental Center, Inc.
sampleRecords.part_of_body: Chest, except internal location of diseases or disorders
sampleRecords.event_description: Struck or run over by rolling powered vehicle
sampleRecords.final_narrative: An employee was working to attach a rotary cutter to the tow hitch of a tractor to then bring it back to the shop. The tractor started to roll away. The employee went to get in the cab of the tractor to stop it from rolling but he tripped and fell. The left rear wheel of the tractor rolled over him. The employee was hospitalized with bruised ribs and shortness of breath.

sampleRecords.event_description: Compressed or pinched by shifting objects or equipment
sampleRecords.final_narrative: An employee was unloading a trailer. His fingertip was pinched between wood and the trailer. The fingertip was amputated.

sampleRecords.event_description: Other fall to lower level less than 6 feet
sampleRecords.final_narrative: An employee fell 2 feet from the top of a cabinet. His wedding ring got caught on the cabinet's frame while falling, causing the amputation of his left ring finger.

sampleRecords.part_of_body: Abdomen, except internal location of diseases or disorders
sampleRecords.nature: Internal injuries to organs and blood vessels of the trunk
sampleRecords.event_description: Other fall to lower level 6 to 10 feet
sampleRecords.final_narrative: An employee was climbing down a ladder after installing a sign. The ladder's leg slipped on muddy ground and the employee fell about 6 feet to the ground, suffering a broken wrist and lacerated liver.
